query: USMCA joint review July 1 2026 outcome - did US provide written notice of withdrawal under Article 34.6? Latest news on Trump USMCA withdrawal or renewal decision June-July 2026
answer:
## Summary

**No — the United States did NOT provide written notice of withdrawal under Article 34.6.**

The July 1, 2026 outcome was a **non-renewal under Article 34.7** (the joint-review/term-extension provision), which is legally and procedurally distinct from the Article 34.6 withdrawal mechanism.

**What happened:**
- USTR Ambassador Jamieson Greer stated the US "did not agree to renew the USMCA in its current form. As a result, the USMCA is not renewed" — but confirmed "the Agreement remains in force."
- Mexico and Canada both wanted to extend for another 16 years; only the US declined.
- This triggered **Article 34.7.4 → annual joint reviews** each year through July 1, 2036.

**Why it's NOT a withdrawal:**
- Article 34.6 requires a formal written notice with a **6-month effective period** — this was never filed.
- The CRS, White & Case, and Mexican firm Basham all explicitly state the joint review is *separate* from the Article 34.6 withdrawal/denunciation process.
- The treaty stays fully in force (tariffs, rules of origin, investment protections all intact), and a 16-year extension can still be agreed "at any time" before 2036.

**Trajectory:** US-Mexico bilateral talks were scheduled for the week of July 20, 2026 in Mexico City. Greer is reportedly seeking bilateral "protocols" layered on the existing deal. No termination is imminent — the earliest automatic expiration would be July 1, 2036.

The full structured findings, sources, and data points have been recorded via the StructuredOutput tool.

sources.title: Ambassador Greer Issues Statement on the USMCA Joint Review
sources.snippet: The United States did not agree to renew the USMCA in its current form. As a result, the USMCA is not renewed... the Agreement remains in force pending resolution of these issues or until the Agreement's termination.

sources.title: USMCA 2026 Joint Review: United States declines to extend Agreement, triggering annual reviews
sources.snippet: The United States declined to confirm its intention to extend the Agreement... This decision triggers the annual joint review process under Article 34.7.4... the Agreement has neither lapsed nor expired.

sources.title: USMCA Joint Review: Process and Role of Congress (CRS Report R48787)
sources.snippet: The joint review process is separate from USMCA's withdrawal provisions, which allow any party to withdraw from the agreement with six months' written notice to the other parties. USMCA, art. 34.6.

sources.title: USMCA Chapter 34 Final Provisions (Articles 34.6 and 34.7 text)
sources.snippet: Article 34.6: Withdrawal. A Party may withdraw from this Agreement by providing written notice of withdrawal to the other Parties. A withdrawal shall take effect six months after a Party provides written notice.

sources.title: Implications of the United States' decision not to extend the validity of the USMCA
sources.snippet: It is important to note that the joint review is distinct from the denunciation process outlined in Article 34.6 of the USMCA. Denunciation occurs when a Party notifies its intention to withdraw... effective six months after it is deposited.

sources.title: Trump Administration Decides Not To Extend USMCA for Another Term: What Does That Mean Exactly?
sources.snippet: A failure to 'confirm a wish/desire to extend' at the 6-year mark kind of sounds like a decision to end the agreement, but it actually has a very different impact in practice... a temporary state of affairs that could be reconsidered down the road.

sources.title: Trump administration declines to renew USMCA trade deal
sources.snippet: Under the terms of the USMCA, non-renewal means the treaty remains in effect but moves into a period of annual reviews for the next 10 years.

sources.title: US declines to extend North American trade deal
sources.snippet: The decision keeps the agreement in place for another 10 years with annual reviews before it expires, unless the three countries agree to renew it with changes. US goods trade deficits with Mexico and Canada reached $197 billion and $48.3 billion in 2025.

sources.title: Trump Administration Decides Against Renewing USMCA, Opts for Annual Review Process
sources.snippet: On July 1, the United States formally declined to renew the USMCA in its current form, triggering an annual review process that extends negotiations for up to a decade.

key_facts: On July 1, 2026, the US declined to renew/extend USMCA for a new 16-year term during the mandatory six-year joint review; it did NOT file an Article 34.6 withdrawal notice.
USTR Ambassador Jamieson Greer stated the US 'did not agree to renew the USMCA in its current form. As a result, the USMCA is not renewed' but 'the Agreement remains in force.'
The action was taken under Article 34.7 (Review and Term Extension), which is legally distinct from Article 34.6 (Withdrawal). No written notice of withdrawal was provided.
Article 34.6 would require six months' written notice to withdraw; this mechanism was NOT invoked.
The US non-confirmation triggered Article 34.7.4: annual joint reviews now occur each year through July 1, 2036.
USMCA remains fully in force; all tariffs preferences, rules of origin, investment protections, and dispute settlement mechanisms are unaffected.
Mexico and Canada both confirmed they wished to extend the agreement for another 16 years; only the US declined.
The 16-year extension is deferred, not lost: parties can agree 'at any time' before 2036 to extend via written head-of-government confirmation, with no renegotiation required.
A third round of US-Mexico bilateral negotiations was scheduled for the week of July 20, 2026 in Mexico City.
USTR Greer is reportedly seeking separate bilateral 'protocols' layered atop the trilateral deal, potentially avoiding new congressional approval.
USMCA entered into force July 1, 2020 and is scheduled to terminate July 1, 2036 if no extension is agreed.

data_points.metric: USMCA scheduled termination date if never extended
